The main computer has a plug-and-play function for add-on systems; proposals include an external jamming pod, and new Israeli air-to-air missiles and guided bombs in the internal weapon bays. Due to unique component requirements, the F-35I's wings are locally manufactured.[413][414][415][416] A senior IAF official said that the F-35's stealth may be partly overcome within 10 years despite a 30 to 40-year service life, thus Israel's insistence on using its own electronic warfare systems.[417] In 2010, Israel Aerospace Industries (IAI) considered a two-seat F-35 concept; an IAI executive noted that there was a "known demand for two seats not only from Israel but from other air forces."[418] In 2008, IAI planned to produce conformal fuel tanks, as well as stealthy external fuel tanks;[419][420] these were reportedly used during the June 2025 Israeli airstrikes against Iran.[375]

It is widely speculated that the F-35I may have the capability to operate as a delivery vehicle for Israeli nuclear weapons, as it is a derivative of the nuclear-capable F-35A.[421][422][423] In 2018, Israeli Channel 2 reported in 2014 that, following Lockheed Martin's nuclear-capable upgrades, allowing some F-35As' weapons systems and software to interface with US permissive action links, a senior US official had declined to comment on whether Israel had requested the same upgrade to its F-35Is.[424]

Proposed variants

[edit]

CF-35

[edit]
Main article: Lockheed Martin F-35 Lightning II Canadian procurement

The Canadian CF-35 was a proposed variant that would differ from the F-35A through the addition of a drogue parachute and the potential inclusion of an F-35B/C-style refueling probe.[404][425] In 2012, it was revealed that the CF-35 would employ the same boom refueling system as the F-35A.[426] One alternative proposal would have been the adoption of the F-35C for its probe refueling and lower landing speed; however, the Parliamentary Budget Officer's report cited the F-35C's limited performance and payload as being too high a price to pay.[427] Following the 2015 Federal Election, the Liberal Party, whose campaign had included a pledge to cancel the F-35 procurement,[428] formed a new government and commenced an open competition to replace the existing CF-18 Hornet.[429] The CF-35 variant was deemed too expensive to develop, and was never considered. The Canadian government decided not to pursue any other modifications in the Future Fighter Capability Project, and instead focused on the potential procurement of the existing F-35A variant.[430]

On 28 March 2022, the Canadian Government began negotiations with Lockheed Martin for 88 F-35As[431] to replace the aging fleet of CF-18 fighters starting in 2025.[432] The aircraft are reported to cost up to CA$19bn total with a life-cycle cost estimated at CA$77bn over the course of the F-35 program.[433][434] On 9 January 2023, Canada formally confirmed the purchase of 88 aircraft. The initial delivery to the Royal Canadian Air Force in 2026 will be 4 aircraft, followed by 6 aircraft each in 2027–2028, and the rest to be delivered by 2032.[435][436] The additional characteristics confirmed for Canadian F-35As included the drag chute pod for landings at short/icy arctic runways, as well as the 'sidekick' system, which allows the CF-35 to carry up to 6 x AIM-120D missiles internally (instead of the typical internal capacity of 4 x AIM-120 missiles on other variants).[437]

New export variant

[edit]

In December 2021, it was reported that Lockheed Martin was developing a new variant for an unspecified foreign customer. The Department of Defense released US$49 million in funding for this work.[438]

Operators

[edit]
Main articles: Lockheed Martin F-35 Lightning II operators and Lockheed Martin F-35 Lightning II procurement
  Operates F-35A, F-35B and F-35C variants (United States)
  Operates F-35A and F-35B variants (Italy, Japan)
  Operates F-35A variant only (Australia, Denmark, Netherlands, Norway, Poland, South Korea)
  Operates F-35B variant and awaiting F-35A delivery (United Kingdom)
  Operates F-35I variant only (Israel)
  Awaiting delivery (F-35A: Belgium, Canada, Czech Republic, Finland, Germany, Greece, Romania, Switzerland; F-35A & F-35B: Singapore)


 Australia
  • Royal Australian Air Force – All 72 F-35A ordered were delivered as of December 2024[update].[356]
 Belgium
  • Belgian Air Force – 12 delivered, of which 8 trainer aircraft are based at Luke Air Force Base in the USA.[439][440] 34 F-35A planned in 2018 as of 2019[update],[441][442] order for 11 additional F-35A pending.[443]
 Denmark
  • Royal Danish Air Force – 21 F-35As delivered (including 6 stationed at Luke Air Force Base for training) of the 43 planned for the RDAF as of October 2025[update].[444][445][446][447][448][397][449]
 Israel
  • Israeli Air Force – 48 F-35I "Adir" delivered as of January 2026.[450][451] Includes one F-35 testbed aircraft for indigenous Israeli weapons, electronics, and structural upgrades, designated AS-15.[452][453] A total of 75 ordered.[454]
 Italy
  • Italian Air Force – 24 F-35As and 8 F-35Bs delivered as of April 2025[update],[455] of 75 F-35As and 20 F-35Bs ordered for the Italian Air Force.[456][457][458][459][460]
  • Italian Navy – 6 delivered as of September 2024[update], of 20 F-35Bs ordered for the Italian Navy.[456][457][458][460][386]
 Japan
  • Japan Air Self-Defense Force – 47 F-35As and F-35Bs delivered and operational as of December 2025[461][462] with a total order of 147, including 105 F-35As and 42 F-35Bs.[463][464][465][466]
 Netherlands
  • Royal Netherlands Air and Space Force – 48 F-35As delivered and operational, of which 8 trainer aircraft are based at Luke Air Force Base in the US.[400] 58 F-35As ordered in total.[467][468][469] The RNLASF is the second air force with a 5th gen-only fighter fleet after the retirement of its F-16s.[470]
 Norway
  • Royal Norwegian Air Force – 52 F-35A delivered.[471] They differ from other F-35As through the addition of a drogue parachute.[472]
 Poland
  • Polish Air Force – 32 F-35A "Husarz" Block 4 jets with "Technology Refresh 3" software update and drogue parachutes ordered, with 8 delivered as of 2025.[473][474][475][476][477][478][479] There are plans to order an additional 32 F-35s, which will comprise two squadrons.[480] The first domestic flights of the F-35 by Polish pilots took place in February 2025, signaling the start of the country's use of the aircraft.[479]
 South Korea
  • Republic of Korea Air Force – 40 F-35As ordered and delivered as of January 2022[update],[481] with 25 more ordered in September 2023.[482][483][484][485]
 United Kingdom
  • Royal Air Force and Royal Navy (owned by the RAF but jointly operated) – 48 F-35Bs received, of which 47 are operational after the loss of one aircraft in November 2021.[486][487][488][343][489][490][491] As of 2018, three of the UK's aircraft were in the US being used for testing and training.[492] The first order of 48 was completed in 2026. A total of 138 are planned to be procured during the lifetime of the program.[493] Whilst the UK was originally expected to only order the F-35B, in June 2025 the UK announced plans to procure 12 F-35As, enabling the UK to join NATO's nuclear mission due to the A variant's ability to carry B61 nuclear bombs internally, alongside 15 F-35Bs.[347][349] With the completion of the initial order of 48 aircraft, a secondary order of 27 aircraft (12 F-35A and 15 F-35B) is expected to be procured by 2033.[494][495]
 United States
  • United States Air Force – 400+ delivered, with 1,763 F-35As planned.[496]
  • United States Marine Corps – 112 F-35B/C delivered,[497] with 280 F-35Bs and 140 F-35Cs planned.[498]
  • United States Navy – 110+ delivered,[497] with 273 F-35Cs planned.[499]

Future operators

[edit]
 Canada
  • Royal Canadian Air Force – 88 F-35As (Block 4) ordered on 9 January 2023. Canada has paid for 30 aircraft to be delivered between 2026 and 2029.[500] The plan was for the remainder to be delivered by 2032, but Canada decided in early 2025 to review the remaining 58 aircraft, possibly replacing them with Saab Gripen. A preliminary audit indicated that the program’s cost had escalated to $27.7 billion from an initial $19 billion.[501][502] The aircraft are to replace CF-18s delivered in the 1980s.[503][504]
Czech Republic
  • Czech Air Force – On 29 June 2023, the U.S. State Department announced the approval of a possible sale to the Czech Republic of F-35 aircraft, munitions and related equipment worth up to $5.62 billion.[505] On 29 January 2024, the Czech government signed a memorandum of understanding with the U.S. to buy 24 F-35As.[506] In September 2024, the Czech Republic signed a contract for F-35A logistics support.[507]
 Finland
  • Finnish Air Force – In 2022, ordered 64 F-35A Block 4s[508] via the HX Fighter Program to replace F/A-18 Hornets.[509][510]
 Germany
  • German Air Force – In 2022, ordered 35 F-35As[511][512] for delivery starting in 2026.[513] As of 2024, an order for an additional 10 was being considered.[514] German F-35s will also replace the older Panavia Tornados in carrying the B61 nuclear bomb.[513]
Greece
  • Hellenic Air Force – In 2024, Greece ordered 20 F-35As for delivery in late 2027 to early 2028,[515][516] with an option to buy 20 more.[517] The Greek F-35A production for 20 aircraft will "begin" with Lot 20, with full production starting in 2027, first delivery at the end of 2028, while the arrival of the first aircraft in Greece is expected for 2030, as per the VP for Strategy and Business Development and the VP for Customer Requirements of Lockheed Martin Aeronautics.[518][519]
 Romania
  • Romanian Air Force – Romania signed a contract for 32 F-35As worth $6.5 billion on 21 November 2024,[520] planning to buy 48 F-35A aircraft in two phases – a first phase of 32 and a second phase of 16. The first F-35s will arrive after 2030 and will replace the current Romanian F-16 fleet between 2034 and 2040.[521][522]
 Singapore
  • Republic of Singapore Air Force – 8 F-35As and 12 F-35Bs on order as of February 2024[update]. The first 4 F-35Bs are to be delivered in 2026, while the other 8 are to be delivered in 2028. The 8 F-35As are expected to arrive by 2030.[523][524]
 Switzerland
  • Swiss Air Force – 36 F-35A ordered to replace the current F-5E/F Tiger II and F/A-18C/D Hornet. Deliveries will begin in 2027 and conclude in 2030.[525][526] In September 2025, it was announced that Lockheed failed to meet the contract price of $7.55B, and that it would be increased by $1.63B.[527] As a result, the Swiss decided to reduce the order to a lower number of planes in December 2025.[528] In January 2026, it was reported that the support infrastructure was also behind schedule and over budget, according to Switzerland's federal auditors.[529]

Potential operators

[edit]
 Morocco
  • Royal Moroccan Air Force – Morocco has entered into negotiations with the United States for the acquisition of the F-35 Lightning II stealth multirole fighter for its air force. These discussions have been ongoing for several months, with documents indicating that Israel has granted its approval in principle for the sale. This authorization is provided within the framework of the Qualitative Military Edge (QME), a clause of the Arms Export Control Act requiring that no arms sale in the region compromise Israel's military superiority. If finalized, the agreement would make Morocco the first African and Arab nation to operate the F-35.[530]
 Saudi Arabia
  • Royal Saudi Air Force – It was announced in November 2025 that Saudi Arabia would procure 48 F-35A fighters. It is likely that the F-35As operated by the RSAF will lack some more advanced features found on Israel's fleet to maintain the Qualitative Military Edge, with some American missiles not to be offered to Saudi Arabia.[531][532][533]

Cancelled operators

[edit]
 Republic of China
  • Republic of China Air Force – Taiwan has repeatedly expressed interest in buying the F-35 to deter and fight off any Chinese attempt to seize the island by force. It is reportedly most interested in the F-35B STOVL variant, which could enable the Republic of China Air Force to continue operations if China bombed the island's runways.[534] But the U.S. has repeatedly rebuffed this interest[535] – for example, in March 2009, September 2011,[536] early 2017[537] and March 2018. The usual reason given is to prevent provoking Beijing. But in April 2018, another reason for U.S. reluctance surfaced: concern that Chinese spies within the Taiwanese Armed Forces might gain classified data about the aircraft. In November 2018, it was reported that Taiwanese military leaders had abandoned efforts to buy the F-35 and would instead buy a larger number of F-16V Viper aircraft. The decision was reportedly motivated by concerns about industry independence, cost, and espionage.[538]
 Egypt
  • Egyptian Air Force – Despite Donald Trump's announcement to sell 20 F-35A fighters to Egypt in 2018, strong objections from the U.S. Department of Defense and Israel led to the deal's cancellation.[539]

 India

  • Indian Air Force – In February 2025, U.S. President Donald Trump offered the F-35 to Prime Minister Narendra Modi of India,[540] which as of March 2025, was also mulling a competing offer from Russia's Sukhoi Su-57.[541] However, in late July 2025, Bloomberg reported that India has already rejected its requirement of the aircraft. While the Indian Ministry of Defence has not confirmed any of this, the Indian Ministry of External Affairs maintained that India has not held any formal discussions about the aircraft's procurement with the USA.[542][543]
Spain
  • Spanish Air and Space Force – The Spanish government explored the acquisition of the F-35 through requests for information and preliminary analysis,[544] but in August 2025 the option was formally ruled out.[545]
 Thailand
  • Royal Thai Air Force – 8 or 12 planned to replace F-16A/B Block 15 ADF in service. On 12 January 2022, Thailand's cabinet approved a budget for the first four F-35A, estimated at 13.8 billion baht in FY2023.[546][547][548] On 22 May 2023, the United States Department of Defense implied it will turn down Thailand's bid to buy F-35 fighters, and instead offer F-16 Block 70/72 Viper and F-15EX Eagle II fighters, a Royal Thai Air Force source said.[549]
 Turkey
  • On 11 July 2002, Turkey became a Level 3 partner of the F-35 Joint Strike Fighter (JSF) development program, and on 25 January 2007, Turkey officially joined the production phase of the JSF program, agreeing to purchase a total of 116 F-35 Lightning II aircraft (100 F-35A CTOL for the Turkish Air Force and 16 F-35B STOVL for the TCG Anadolu).[550] Future purchases have been banned by the U.S. following Turkey's decision to buy the S-400 missile system from Russia, with contracts canceled by early 2020.[551] Six of Turkey's 30 ordered F-35As were completed as of 2019[update] (they are still kept in a hangar in the United States as of 2023[update][552][553] and so far haven't been transferred to the USAF, despite a modification in the 2020 Fiscal Year defense budget by the U.S. Congress which gives authority to do so if necessary),[554][555] and two more were at the assembly line in 2020.[554][555] The first four F-35As were delivered to Luke Air Force Base in 2018[556] and 2019[557] for the training of Turkish pilots.[558][559] On 20 July 2020, the U.S. government had formally approved the seizure of eight F-35As originally bound for Turkey and their transfer to the USAF, together with a contract to modify them to USAF specifications.[560] The U.S. has not refunded the $1.4 billion payment made by Turkey for purchasing the F-35A fighters as of January 2023[update].[552][553] On 1 February 2024, the United States expressed willingness to readmit Turkey into the F-35 program if Turkey agrees to give up its S-400 system.[561] After Trump and Erdoğan's phone call in March 2025, news was reported in the press that Trump could approve the sale of F-35s to Turkey if Turkey resolves the S-400 issue.[562] Following a meeting between U.S. President Trump and Turkish President Erdoğan at the White House on September 25, 2025, it was revealed that one of the proposed solutions, reported by the media in October 2025, stipulates the relocation of Turkey's S-400 missile systems to the Nakhchivan Autonomous Republic, an exclave of Azerbaijan which has a five-mile border with Turkey, as a precondition for lifting the CAATSA sanctions and approving the sale and transfer of F-35s and Patriot surface-to-air missile systems to Turkey.[563] On 24 June 2026, during a joint press conference with NATO Secretary General Mark Rutte at the White House, U.S. President Donald Trump stated that he would "probably do something that will make Turkey very happy" regarding Turkey's request to rejoin the F-35 program, ahead of the NATO Summit in Ankara.[564] The same day, Reuters reported that the U.S. administration was expected to approve the export of General Electric F110 engines for Turkey's KAAN fighter program before the summit, despite opposition in U.S. Congress.[565][566] Turkey's Ministry of National Defense announced that diplomatic consultations with U.S. counterparts on the country's potential return to the F-35 program and the removal of related restrictions were continuing. U.S. officials, however, emphasized that any policy shift would depend on addressing concerns regarding Turkey's possession of the Russian S-400 air defense system.[567][568][569]
 United Arab Emirates
  • United Arab Emirates Air Force – Up to 50 F-35As planned.[570] On 27 January 2021, the Biden administration temporarily suspended the F-35 sales to the UAE.[571] After pausing the bill to review the sale, the Biden administration confirmed it would move forward with the deal on 13 April 2021.[572] In December 2021, the UAE withdrew from purchasing F-35s as they did not agree to the additional terms of the transaction from the US.[573][574] On 14 September 2024, a senior UAE official said that the United Arab Emirates does not expect to resume talks with the U.S. about the F-35.[575]

Accidents and notable incidents

[edit]
Main article: List of accidents and incidents involving the Lockheed Martin F-35 Lightning II

The F-35 is a statistically safe military aircraft, described as being twice as safe as the F-16.[576] Still, since 2014, more than a dozen have crashed or otherwise been involved in incidents that have killed or severely injured people or destroyed the aircraft. Some were caused by operator error; others by mechanical problems, some of which set the entire program back.[577]

As of January 2025[update], there were 11 crashes causing the loss of an F-35 airframe, primarily in the US.[394]

The only known fatal crash of an F-35 occurred on 9 April 2019, when a Japan Air Self-Defense Force F-35A crashed in the Pacific Ocean east of Aomori Prefecture at an estimated speed of Mach 0.9.[394] The accident report attributed the cause to the pilot's spatial disorientation. The pilot's remains and the flight recorder were salvaged, although no data could be retrieved, while the wreckage itself was not salvaged.[392]

On 19 March 2026, during the 2026 Iran war, a US F-35 flying a combat mission over Iran was forced to make an emergency landing at a regional air base.[310] It was the F-35's first combat-related emergency landing. If confirmed, it would be the first Iranian interception of a US aircraft.[311] A US Central Command spokesman said the aircraft "landed safely, and the pilot is in stable condition".[310] The Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps said it had "seriously damaged" the F-35 over central Iran.[312][313] Air & Space Forces Magazine reported the aircraft had taken ground fire,[314] and that the pilot had suffered shrapnel wounds.[315] CNN reported damage from "what is believed to be Iranian fire".[316] An NPR correspondent reported that the plane had made a "hard landing" and would not return to service soon.[317] Iran's Tasnim News Agency released a video purporting to show the interception,[312][313][318] perhaps by a low-quality infrared search and track system,[319][320][314] although some analysts suspect a deepfake.[319]

Specifications (F-35A)

[edit]

Data from Lockheed Martin: F-35 specifications,[578][579][580][581] Lockheed Martin: F-35 weaponry,[582] Lockheed Martin: F-35 Program Status,[123] F-35 Program brief,[174] FY2019 Select Acquisition Report (SAR),[410] Director of Operational Test & Evaluation[583]

General characteristics

  • Crew: 1
  • Length: 51.4 ft (15.7 m)
  • Wingspan: 35 ft (11 m)
  • Height: 14.4 ft (4.4 m)
  • Wing area: 460 sq ft (43 m2)
  • Aspect ratio: 2.66
  • Empty weight: 29,300 lb (13,290 kg)
  • Gross weight: 49,540 lb (22,471 kg)
  • Max takeoff weight: 65,918 lb (29,900 kg) [584]
  • Fuel capacity: 18,250 lb (8,278 kg) internal
  • Powerplant: 1 × Pratt & Whitney F135-PW-100 afterburning turbofan, 28,000 lbf (120 kN) thrust dry, 43,000 lbf (190 kN) with afterburner

Performance

  • Maximum speed: Mach 1.6 at high altitude (1,228 mph / 1,976 km/h)
    • Mach 1.06, 700 knots (806 mph; 1,296 km/h) at sea level
  • Range: 1,500 nmi (1,700 mi, 2,800 km)
  • Combat range: 669 nmi (770 mi, 1,239 km) interdiction mission (air-to-surface) on internal fuel
    • 760 nmi (870 mi; 1,410 km), air-to-air configuration on internal fuel[585]
  • Service ceiling: 50,000 ft (15,000 m)
  • g limits: +9.0
  • Wing loading: 107.7 lb/sq ft (526 kg/m2) at gross weight
  • Thrust/weight: 0.87 at gross weight (1.07 at loaded weight with 50% internal fuel)

Armament

  • Guns: 1 × 25 mm GAU-22/A 4-barrel rotary cannon, 180 rounds[N 18]
  • Hardpoints: 4 × internal stations, 6 × external stations on wings with a capacity of 5,700 pounds (2,600 kg) internal, 15,000 pounds (6,800 kg) external, 18,000 pounds (8,200 kg) total weapons payload, with provisions to carry combinations of:
    • Missiles:
      • Air-to-air missiles:
        • AIM-9X Sidewinder
        • AIM-120 AMRAAM
        • AIM-132 ASRAAM
        • AIM-260 JATM (being integrated)[586]
        • MBDA Meteor (Block 4, for F-35B, not before 2027)[587][202][588]
      • Air-to-surface missiles:
        • AGM-88G AARGM-ER (Block 4)
        • AGM-158 JASSM[193]
        • SPEAR 3 (Block 4, in development, integration contracted)[186][588]
        • AGM-88J[citation needed] Stand-in Attack Weapon (SiAW)[589]
      • Anti-ship missiles:
        • AGM-158C LRASM[590] (being integrated)
        • Joint Strike Missile (being integrated)[591]
        • Hypersonic Attack Cruise Missile (Australia; after 2027)
    • Bombs:
      • Joint Direct Attack Munition
      • Paveway
      • Precision-guided glide bomb:
        • AGM-154 JSOW
        • GBU-39 Small Diameter Bomb
        • GBU-53/B StormBreaker
      • B61 mod 12 nuclear bomb[592][187]

Avionics

  • AN/APG-81 or AN/APG-85 (Lot 17 onwards) AESA radar[593][594]
  • AN/AAQ-40 Electro-Optical Targeting System[595]
  • AN/AAQ-37 Electro-Optical Distributed Aperture System[596]
  • AN/ASQ-239 Barracuda electronic warfare/electronic countermeasures system[597]
  • AN/ASQ-242 CNI suite, which includes
    • Harris Corporation Multifunction Advanced Data Link (MADL) communication system
    • Link 16 data link
    • SINCGARS
    • An IFF interrogator and transponder
    • HAVE QUICK
    • AM, VHF, UHF AM, and UHF FM Radio
    • GUARD survival radio
    • A radar altimeter
    • An instrument landing system
    • A TACAN system
    • Instrument carrier landing system
    • A JPALS
    • TADIL-J JVMF/VMF

[edit]

See also

[edit]
  • Aviation portal

Related development

  • Lockheed Martin X-35 – Concept demonstrator aircraft for Joint Strike Fighter program

Aircraft of comparable role, configuration, and era

  • Lockheed Martin F-22 Raptor – American fifth-generation air superiority fighter
  • Chengdu J-20 – Chinese fifth-generation fighter aircraft
  • Shenyang J-35 – Chinese fifth-generation fighter aircraft
  • Sukhoi Su-57 – Russian fifth-generation fighter aircraft
  • TAI TF Kaan – Turkish fifth-generation fighter under development by Turkish Aerospace Industries
  • KAI KF-21 Boramae – Advanced multirole fighter aircraft under development by South Korea and Indonesia

  • List of fighter aircraft

Notes

[edit]
  1. ↑ As of August 2023[update], the program was 80% over budget and 10 years late.[10]
  2. ↑ The F-35A is only certified to carry the B61 Mod 12 variant, with a maximum yield of 50 kilotons.[17]
  3. ↑ Lockheed acquired General Dynamics fighter division at Fort Worth in 1993 and merged with Martin Marietta in 1995 to form Lockheed Martin.
  4. ↑ As these were concept demonstrator aircraft for risk reduction, they did not need to have the internal structure or most subsystems of the final aircraft as a weapon system.
  5. ↑ The F-35 swivel nozzle design was pioneered by the Convair Model 200.[27]
  6. ↑ The thrust vectoring nozzle would eventually be replaced by an axisymmetric low-observable nozzle to reduce weight.
  7. ↑ FACO is also performed in Italy and Japan for some partner and export customers as part of the industrial benefits from international cooperation.
  8. ↑ This first prototype lacked the weight optimization from SWAT.
  9. ↑ Early F-35Bs have a service life as low as 2,100 hours before retrofits as seen on Lot 9 and later aircraft.
  10. ↑ Adaptive cycle engine technology had been under development under Air Force Life Cycle Management Center's (AFLCMC) Adaptive Engine Transition Program (AETP) and its precursors.
  11. ↑ Turkey was the sole supplier of several F-35 parts, thus forcing the program to find replacement vendors.[103]
  12. ↑ The F-35C has additional ailerons at the folding sections of the wings.
  13. ↑ In 2014, Michael Gilmore, Director of Operational Test & Evaluation, stated that "software development, integration in the contractor labs, and delivery of mature capability to flight test continued to be behind schedule."[134]
  14. ↑ Rockwell Collins and Elbit Systems formed the joint venture Vision Systems International (VSI), later renamed Collins Elbit Vision Systems (CEVS).
  15. ↑ In 2002, solid-state laser weapons were reportedly being developed for the F-35.[207][208][209]
  16. ↑ "Bubbling and blistering" of the horizontal tails and tail booms were observed once during flutter tests of the F-35B and C in late 2011; according to the program office, the problem has only occurred once despite numerous attempts to replicate it, and an improved spray-on coating has been implemented since as a mitigation measure. On 17 December 2019, the Pentagon program office closed the issue with no further actions planned, and instead is imposing a time limit on high-speed flight for the F-35B and C to reduce the risk of damaging the stealth coatings and antennas located on the back of the aircraft.[260][261]
  17. ↑ Wing drop is an uncommanded roll that can occur during high-g transsonic maneuvering.
  18. ↑ F-35B and F-35C have the cannon in an external pod with 220 rounds.

  376. ↑ Wells, Peter; Russell, George; Wheatley, Jonathan; Georgiadis, Philip; White, Alexandra (13 June 2025). "Israel-Iran day 1 as it happened: Iran retaliates after Israel launches major attack; Trump warns Tehran of 'even more brutal' attacks without nuclear deal". Financial Times. Retrieved 7 July 2025.
  377. ↑ "Iran claims it shot down Israeli F-35; IDF unaware of any losses". The Times of Israel. ISSN 0040-7909. Retrieved 7 July 2025.
  378. ↑ Wall, Robert; Trimble, Steve (28 February 2026). "Israel Joins U.S. 'Operation Epic Fury' In New Fighting With Iran". Aviation Week & Space Technology. Retrieved 28 February 2026.
  379. ↑ Slayton, Nicholas (28 February 2026). "US launches 'major combat operations' against Iran". Task & Purpose. Retrieved 28 February 2026.
  380. ↑ Newdick, Thomas (4 March 2026). "Israeli Air Force First To Claim F-35 Air-To-Air Kill Of A Crewed Aircraft". The War Zone. Retrieved 5 March 2026.
  381. ↑ "Israel F-35 downs Iranian fighter jet in first war dogfight". The Jerusalem Post. 4 March 2026.
  382. ↑ Kimon, Elisha Ben (4 March 2026). "Historic moment over Tehran: Israeli F-35 shoots down Iranian fighter jet". Ynetglobal. Retrieved 4 March 2026.
  383. ↑ "Israeli Air Force F-35 shoots down Russian-made Iranian jet over Tehran". The Times of Israel. 4 March 2026. ISSN 0040-7909. Retrieved 4 March 2026.
  384. ↑ Bob, Yonah Jeremy. "As US F-35 stealth fighters are struggling, do Israel's remain combat-ready? Multiple Israeli sources said that the Jewish state's F-35s have remained more combat-ready than the report detailed for American F-35s, though none were willing to provide specific numbers.", The Jerusalem Post, June 17, 2026. Accessed June 17, 2026. "After a US watchdog report earlier this week said that the American fleet of F-35 stealth fighters has seen reduced readiness and usability as maintenance issues drag down mission-capable rates, Israeli sources said that their F-35s were faring better. Multiple Israeli sources said that the IAF’s F-35s have remained more combat-ready than what was detailed about American F-35s in the report, with one source even saying that over the course of the wars since 2023, the F-35 has remained operationally ready 90% of the time. Two different sources suggested that Israeli maintenance standards may be higher.... The GAO report said that the mission-capable rate for one mission fell from 67% to 44% between 2021 and 2025, while the full mission-capable rate dropped from 38% to 25%."
  385. ↑ Kington, Tom (30 November 2018). "Italy F-35s reach initial operating capability". Defense News. Archived from the original on 19 June 2026. Retrieved 13 January 2022.
  386. Jump up to: 1 2 Cenciotti, David (28 August 2024). "Italian Navy Declares Initial Operational Capability For The Embarked F-35B Component". The Aviationist. Retrieved 28 April 2025.

  510. ↑ Ristamäki, Juha; Nurmi, Lauri (5 December 2021). "IL:n tiedot: Puolustusvoimat esittää yhdysvaltalaista F-35:ttä Suomen uudeksi hävittäjäksi" [Information from IL: The Defense Forces presents the US F-35 as Finland's new fighter]. Iltalehti (in Finnish). Archived from the original on 10 October 2023.
  511. ↑ Sabak, Juliusz (29 July 2022). "Niemcy: Wielki zakup F-35 z uzbrojeniem za 8,4 mld dolarów" [Germany: Major purchase of F-35 with weapons for $8.4 billion]. defence24.pl (in Polish). Archived from the original on 10 October 2023.
  512. ↑ "Germany Becomes Latest Country to Join the F-35 Lightning II Global Team". Lockheed Martin. 14 December 2022. Archived from the original on 10 October 2023. Retrieved 15 December 2022.
  513. Jump up to: 1 2 Greet, Kai (26 March 2025). "Germany Doubles Down on F-35 Procurement". The Aviationist. Retrieved 28 April 2025.
  514. ↑ "Germany Is in Talks to Buy 10 Additional F-35 Fighter Jets". Bloomberg, 7 June 2024.
  515. ↑ Lee, Matthew (27 January 2024). "US approves sale of F-16 jets to Turkey, F-35s to Greece". Defense News. Retrieved 2 February 2024.
  516. ↑ "Greece signs deal to buy 20 US-made F-35 jets in major military overhaul". AP News. 25 July 2024. Retrieved 16 August 2024.
  517. ↑ "Greece Becomes Newest Member Of The F-35 Lightning II Global Alliance". news.lockheedmartin.com. 25 July 2024.
  518. ↑ Ioannidis, Stavros (14 May 2025). F-35: Από το 2027 η παραγωγή των ελληνικών μαχητικών [F-35: Production of the greek fighter aircraft to begin by 2027]. Kathimerini (in Greek). Archived from the original on 21 July 2025. «Η παραγωγή των ελληνικών αεροσκαφών ξεκινά στην Παρτίδα 20, για την οποία έχει αρχίσει η προμήθεια μακροχρόνιων υλικών, ενώ η πλήρης παραγωγή προγραμματίζεται για το 2027. Μετά την παραγωγή, η πρώτη παράδοση αναμένεται στα τέλη του 2028, με την άφιξη του πρώτου αεροσκάφους στην Ελλάδα να έχει προγραμματιστεί για το 2030. Ολα τα αεροσκάφη της Ελλάδας θα είναι στην πιο πρόσφατη διαθέσιμη διαμόρφωση», σημειώνει η κ. Μάδεργουεϊ [...]. ["Production of the Greek aircraft will begin in Lot 20, for which the procurement of long-term materials has already begun, with full production scheduled for 2027. Following production, the first delivery is expected at the end of 2028, with the arrival of the first aircraft in Greece scheduled for 2030. All aircraft in Greece will be in the latest available configuration," notes Ms. Motherway [...].]
  519. ↑ Vasilis, Nethos (15 September 2025). Γκάρι Νορθ στην «Κ»: Το 2028 το πρώτο ελληνικό F-35 [Garry North in "K": In 2028 the first greek F-35]. Kathimerini (in Greek). Archived from the original on 16 September 2025. Η Πολεμική Αεροπορία (Π.Α.), το Γραφείο Κοινού Προγράμματος και η Lockheed Martin ξεκίνησαν επίσημα το ελληνικό πρόγραμμα F-35 τον Μάιο. Η παραγωγή ελληνικών αεροσκαφών ξεκινάει στην παρτίδα 20, για την οποία έχει ξεκινήσει η μακροχρόνια προμήθεια. Η πλήρης παραγωγή έχει προγραμματιστεί για το 2027, με την πρώτη παράδοση να αναμένεται προς το παρόν στα τέλη του 2028. [The Hellenic Air Force (HAF), the Joint Program Office, and Lockheed Martin officially launched the Greek F-35 program in May. Production of Greek aircraft will begin with batch 20, for which long-term procurement has already begun. Full production is scheduled for 2027, with the first delivery currently expected at the end of 2028.]

  539. ↑ admin (12 March 2025). "Egypt Was on Verge of Acquiring F-35s in 2018—Until Israel and Pentagon Fiercely Intervened". Defence Security Asia. Retrieved 10 November 2025.
  540. ↑ "India's Modi agrees to US trade talks, Trump offers F-35 jets during White House meeting". USA Today.
  541. ↑ "Trump says US to increase military sales to India, eventually provide F-35 jets". Reuters.
  542. ↑ Srivastava, Shruti; Sen, Sudhi Ranjan (1 July 2025). "India Weighs Options to Placate Trump After Shock 25% Tariff". Retrieved 4 August 2025.
  543. ↑ "Trade war: No F-35 jets from US for India? Govt mulls next steps after 25% tariff shock". The Times of India. 1 August 2025. ISSN 0971-8257. Retrieved 4 August 2025.
  544. ↑ "120 millones la unidad: por qué comprar el F-35 sería un error (inevitable) para España" [120 million euros per unit: why buying the F-35 would be (inevitably) a mistake for Spain]. El Confidencial (in Spanish). 14 March 2017. Retrieved 18 August 2025.
  545. ↑ "El Gobierno aparca la compra de cazas F-35 estadounidenses y busca alternativas europeas". El Pais (in Spanish). 5 August 2025.
  546. ↑ Nanuam, Wassana (12 January 2022). "Air force eyes B13.8bn jets purchase". Bangkok Post. Archived from the original on 3 October 2025.
  547. ↑ Nanuam, Wassana (31 December 2021). "Air force eyes F-35 stealth jets". Bangkok Post. Archived from the original on 24 February 2026.
  548. ↑ "ผบ.ทอ. ประกาศซื้อ F-35 อีกครั้ง พร้อม MUM-T เผยเสนอในปีงบ 66 นี้เลย" [Air Force Commander announces purchase of F-35 again, with MUM-T revealed to be offered in fiscal year 2023.]. thaiarmedforce (in Thai). 31 December 2021. Archived from the original on 10 October 2023.
  549. ↑ Nanuam, Wassana (22 May 2023). "US Pentagon set to rebuff Thailand's bid for F-35s". Bangkok Post. Archived from the original on 16 November 2025. Retrieved 23 May 2023.
  550. ↑ "Turkey plans to purchase four more F-35 jet fighters and five CH-47F transport helicopters". 8 January 2015. Archived from the original on 22 May 2015. Retrieved 12 January 2015.
  551. ↑ Pawlyk, Oriana (16 June 2019). "Trump: Turkey Will Be Out of F-35 Fighter Jet Program After S-400 Buy". Military.com. Archived from the original on 16 March 2023.
  552. Jump up to: 1 2 Finnerty, Ryan (23 January 2023). "US, Turkish defence officials meet for second round of F-35 discussions". FlightGlobal. Archived from the original on 23 January 2023.
  553. Jump up to: 1 2 Jennings, Gareth (24 January 2023). "US, Turkey continue talks to settle F-35 dispute". Janes. Archived from the original on 3 August 2023.
  554. Jump up to: 1 2 "US to buy eight F-35 jets originally built for Turkey". TRT World. Reuters. 21 July 2020. Archived from the original on 9 December 2023.
  555. Jump up to: 1 2 Insinna, Valerie (21 July 2020). "It's official: US Air Force to buy Turkish F-35s". Defense News. Archived from the original on 19 June 2026.
  556. ↑ "F-35'lerin ilk teslimat töreni ABD'de gerçekleşti" [The first delivery ceremony of F-35s took place in the USA]. trthaber.com (in Turkish). TRT Haber. 21 June 2018. Archived from the original on 10 October 2023.
  557. ↑ Stone, Mike; Ali, Idrees (4 April 2019). "Turkish F-35 delivered to training base in Arizona, official says". Reuters. Archived from the original on 5 April 2023. Retrieved 17 July 2019.
  558. ↑ "Turkish F35 takes flight". YouTube (Video). Kaparzo1453. 23 May 2018. Archived from the original on 4 March 2023. Retrieved 7 March 2023.{{cite web}}: CS1 maint: bot: original URL status unknown (link)
  559. ↑ "Turkish Air Force pilots training with Turkish F-35As at Luke Air Force Base". YouTube (Video). Tonk298. 26 February 2019. Archived from the original on 4 March 2023. Retrieved 7 March 2023.{{cite web}}: CS1 maint: bot: original URL status unknown (link)

  561. ↑ "US ready to greenlight Turkey's F-35 fighter jet program if Ankara gives up Russian S-300s and S-400s". February 2024. Archived from the original on 2 February 2024. Retrieved 3 July 2024.
  562. ↑ "Trump eyes lifting sanctions, potential sale of prized fighter jet to Turkey". Fox News. 21 March 2025. Retrieved 22 March 2025.

Bibliography

[edit]
  • Hamstra, Jeffrey (2019). Hamstra, Jeffrey W. (ed.). The F-35 Lightning II: From Concept to Cockpit. American Institute of Aeronautics and Astronautics. doi:10.2514/4.105678. ISBN 978-1-62410-566-1. S2CID 212996081.
  • Keijsper, Gerald (2007). Lockheed F-35 Joint Strike Fighter. London: Pen & Sword Aviation. ISBN 978-1-84415-631-3.
  • Lake, Jon (December 2010). "The West's Great Hope". AirForces Monthly.
  • Polmar, Norman (2005). The Naval Institute Guide to the Ships and Aircraft of the U.S. Fleet. Annapolis, MD: Naval Institute Press. ISBN 978-1-59114-685-8.

Further reading

[edit]
  • Borgu, Aldo (2004). A Big Deal: Australia's Future Air Combat Capability. Canberra: Australian Strategic Policy Institute. ISBN 1-920722-25-4.
  • Spick, Mike (2002). The Illustrated Directory of Fighters. London: Salamander. ISBN 1-84065-384-1.
  • Winchester, Jim (2005). Concept Aircraft: Prototypes, X-Planes, and Experimental Aircraft. San Diego, CA: Thunder Bay Press. ISBN 978-1-59223-480-6. OCLC 636459025.
